text: Neuroscientist Benjamin Libet's 1983 experiments found that a readiness potential in the motor cortex preceded conscious awareness of the intention to move by roughly 350 milliseconds. Some took this as evidence that free will is an illusion — the brain decides before 'we' do. Libet himself disagreed: he noted that subjects could still veto the movement in the final 150 milliseconds. This 'free won't' — the power to inhibit rather than initiate — may be the more accurate model of attentional freedom. We do not summon focus from nothing; we intercept and redirect pulls that arise unbidden.
question: What did Libet call the attentional freedom he believed survived his experimental findings?
